source_textbook: Token Optimization: Measuring, Analyzing, and Reducing the Cost of Generative AI

questions.question: What is this course about?
questions.answer: Token Optimization teaches engineers and FinOps practitioners to measure, analyze, and reduce the cost of generative AI systems without sacrificing quality. It covers tokenization, vendor pricing, structured logging, observability, A/B testing, prompt caching, RAG tuning, model routing, agent budget policies, and batch APIs across Anthropic Claude, OpenAI, and Google Gemini.

questions.question: Who is this course for?
questions.answer: Software engineers, ML engineers, platform engineers, technical leads, FinOps practitioners, and engineering managers responsible for the cost and performance of GenAI systems in production. Secondary audience is graduate students in CS, data science, or information systems.

questions.question: What will I be able to do after finishing the course?
questions.answer: Instrument LLM applications with structured logging, build cost dashboards, run controlled A/B tests, configure prompt caching, design model-routing layers, and write agent budget policies. Authors Skills that delegate deterministic work to scripts.

questions.question: What do I need to know before starting?
questions.answer: Working knowledge of one programming language (Python preferred), familiarity with REST APIs and JSON, basic command-line and Git skills, and casual experience with LLM tools like Claude or ChatGPT. Helpful but not required: logging frameworks, cloud cost dashboards, experiment tracking. No transformer math required.

questions.question: How is the textbook structured?
questions.answer: 20 chapters progressing from foundations to advanced practice. Chapters 1-3 cover tokens, tokenization, pricing. Chapters 4-6 cover the three vendor ecosystems. Chapters 7-8 cover harnesses and Skills. Chapters 9-11 cover logging, observability, log analysis. Chapter 12 covers A/B testing. Chapters 13-18 cover the major optimization levers. Chapter 19 covers batch and privacy. Chapter 20 is the capstone.

questions.question: How long does it take to complete the course?
questions.answer: Sized for a one-semester graduate course or a four-to-six-week self-paced study at 6-10 hours per week. Each chapter is readable in a single sitting. Capstone takes 15-25 hours including instrumentation, A/B testing, and report.

questions.question: What makes this course different from other LLM courses?
questions.answer: Most LLM courses focus on capability and treat cost as an afterthought. This course inverts that, treating token cost as a first-class engineering concern with measurement and optimization tools. It is deliberately vendor-pluralistic, covering Anthropic, OpenAI, and Google equally.

questions.question: Do I need to pay for API access to follow along?
questions.answer: Yes, but per-example cost is small. Optimization exercises use cheapest-tier models (Haiku, GPT-4o-mini, Gemini Flash) where a chapter costs under one dollar. Batch exercises cost half thanks to batch discount. Capstone can be sized to your budget.

questions.question: How do I navigate the textbook?
questions.answer: Left sidebar lists chapters in order; right sidebar lists section headers. Cross-references link to other chapters, glossary, or MicroSims. Search box jumps to any concept by name. Learning graph shows dependency structure of all 475 concepts.

questions.question: What is a MicroSim and why does this textbook have so many?
questions.answer: A small browser-based interactive simulation embedded in a chapter, usually built with p5.js or Chart.js, that lets you manipulate parameters and see results update in real time. They exist because token economics has nonlinear effects (cache hit rates, batch discounts, output premiums) easier to feel than read about.

questions.question: Where do I find definitions of technical terms?
questions.answer: The glossary defines every technical term used in the textbook with a one-sentence definition and concrete example, covering over 580 entries. When you encounter unfamiliar terms in a chapter, search the glossary first.

questions.question: Who is Pemba and why is there a red panda on every page?
questions.answer: Pemba is the textbook's mascot, a russet-and-cream red panda with engineer's glasses appearing in admonition boxes throughout chapters to flag key concepts, mistakes, and wins. Red pandas eat one bamboo leaf at a time, mirroring how good token engineering compounds small deliberate choices.

questions.question: What is a token?
questions.answer: A token is the atomic unit a large language model reads and writes. Not a character, not a word, but a chunk of text produced by the tokenizer. English prose averages four characters per token, so 1,000 words is around 1,300 tokens. Models bill in tokens.

questions.question: What is the difference between input tokens and output tokens?
questions.answer: Input tokens are everything sent to the model: system prompt, prior turns, current user message, retrieved context, tool definitions, tool results. Output tokens are everything the model generates back, including thinking and tool-call content. Output tokens are billed at three to five times the input rate.

questions.question: Why are output tokens more expensive than input tokens?
questions.answer: Output tokens are autoregressive: the model generates each one sequentially, conditioned on every previous token, with no batching within a single response. Input tokens process in a single forward pass. Generation has heavier compute and latency profile per token.

questions.question: What is a context window?
questions.answer: Maximum total tokens (input plus output) a model processes in a single request. 200K is common for current Claude and GPT; Gemini Pro extends to 1M tokens. A hard ceiling: exceed it and the request fails. Also a soft cost ceiling because long contexts get expensive.

questions.question: What is prompt caching?
questions.answer: A vendor feature that lets you mark stable prompt prefixes so the vendor stores the prefilled state and reuses it on subsequent requests sharing the prefix. Cached input tokens bill at roughly 10% of normal input rate. The single highest-leverage cost optimization in this textbook.

questions.question: What is a tokenizer and why does it matter?
questions.answer: The algorithm that converts text into integer token IDs the model sees. Each model family has its own tokenizer; the same English sentence can produce different token counts on different vendors, sometimes by 20% or more. Cross-vendor cost comparisons require care; tokenizer drift can quietly inflate cost on migration.

questions.question: What is byte-pair encoding?
questions.answer: The most common LLM tokenization algorithm. Starts from individual bytes and iteratively merges the most frequent adjacent pairs in a training corpus, producing a vocabulary of common subwords. Frequent words get one token; rare words split into pieces; unusual sequences split into many tokens.

questions.question: What is a system prompt?
questions.answer: The instruction block at the start of every request telling the model who it is, how to behave, what tools it has, what constraints to respect. Sent on every request, so a bloated 4,000-token system prompt silently multiplies cost. Hygiene is high-leverage cheap optimization.

questions.question: What is the difference between a prompt and a conversation?
questions.answer: A prompt is a single request: system prompt plus user message plus context. A conversation is a sequence of prompts where each new request includes the full prior history as input. The silent compounding cost of multi-turn dialogue: every turn pays input cost on the entire prior history.

questions.question: What is an AI coding harness?
questions.answer: An interactive tool that wraps a language model with file-system access, shell execution, and editor controls so the model can read, modify, and ship code. Three covered: Claude Code, OpenAI Codex CLI, Google Antigravity. Each generates dramatically more tokens per task than a plain chat call.

questions.question: What is an agentic loop?
questions.answer: The control structure where a model issues a tool call, the harness executes it, results return, model issues next call, iterating until stop. Each iteration replays entire prior history as input. A ten-iteration loop pays input-token cost ten times for the same context.

questions.question: What is a Skill?
questions.answer: A unit of harness-loaded capability comprising a short trigger description, a markdown body, and optionally bundled scripts and assets. Only the trigger description sits in context at all times; body and scripts load on demand. Moving deterministic prose into scripts can reduce per-invocation tokens by 30%.

questions.question: How does a harness decide which Skill to invoke?
questions.answer: The harness reads only short trigger descriptions of every Skill, not full bodies, and the model selects which Skill matches the current task based on those descriptions. The selected Skill's body is then lazy-loaded. Trigger description must be specific (avoid false negatives) and short (low baseline cost).

questions.question: What is structured logging for LLM calls?
questions.answer: Emitting a single JSON log line per LLM call with model, prompt hash, input tokens, output tokens, cached tokens, latency, cost, feature tag, user identifier, outcome. Source of truth for every cost question. Privacy-safe variants redact or hash the prompt while keeping metadata.

questions.question: What is cost attribution?
questions.answer: The process of taking raw token-cost events and rolling them up by feature, user, model, prompt template, or business outcome. Answers questions like 'which feature is most expensive per user?' Without attribution you only know the bill total; with it, you know where to optimize.

questions.question: What is the cost-quality tradeoff?
questions.answer: Empirical relationship between how much you spend and how well a task gets done. Cheaper models, shorter prompts, tighter context reduce cost and at some point reduce quality. The job is to find the Pareto frontier where you cannot reduce cost without losing quality.

questions.question: What is A/B testing in the context of prompts and models?
questions.answer: A controlled experiment splitting traffic between control (current prompt/model) and treatment (proposed change), measuring cost, quality, latency, satisfaction on each. Output is a statistically defensible answer separating real cost reductions from noise and catching quality regressions an eye-test would miss.

questions.question: What is RAG and how does it affect cost?
questions.answer: Retrieval-Augmented Generation: a retrieval system finds relevant documents and injects them into the prompt as context. RAG adds input tokens (retrieved chunks) and sometimes reduces output tokens. Cost-optimization questions: chunk size, top-k, and which retrieved chunks to drop because they don't help the answer.

questions.question: What is model routing?
questions.answer: A pattern where a request is first classified by difficulty, then sent to the cheapest model that can handle it (typically Haiku, GPT-4o-mini, Gemini Flash) with an escalation path to a larger model when confidence is low. Well-tuned routing delivers 60-80% cost savings on workloads where most requests are easy.

questions.question: What is a batch API?
questions.answer: An asynchronous endpoint where you submit many requests at once and receive results within a vendor-specified window (typically 24 hours) in exchange for a 50% discount. Ideal for any workload that doesn't need real-time response: nightly classification, bulk evaluation, embedding generation, offline analysis.

questions.question: What is an agent budget policy?
questions.answer: A documented set of limits on agent token consumption (per session, per tool call, per repository, per pull request) enforced by the harness or middleware. Without one, a misbehaving agent can run up four-figure bills overnight. With one, the agent gracefully degrades when budget is exhausted.

questions.question: What is a cache hit rate?
questions.answer: The fraction of input tokens served from prompt cache rather than billed at full rate. Single most important health metric for any cached application. Target above 80% is achievable for features with long stable prefix; below 50% usually means something is silently invalidating the cache key.

questions.question: What is the difference between synchronous, streaming, and batch modes?
questions.answer: Synchronous: send and wait for full response. Streaming: returns tokens as they generate, same cost, better perceived latency. Batch: many requests submitted at once for async processing in delivery window, half the cost but minutes-to-hours of latency. Choice driven by latency requirements, not cost alone.

questions.question: What does it mean to be vendor-pluralistic?
questions.answer: Equal weight to Anthropic Claude, OpenAI, and Google Gemini, teaching patterns that work across all three rather than betting on one. Production systems often mix vendors (cheap classification on one, long-context on another, code on a third) and cost-optimization patterns differ enough to require fluency in all.

questions.question: What is the relationship between latency, cost, and quality?
questions.answer: A triangle no single configuration optimizes simultaneously. Bigger models are usually higher quality but higher cost and latency. Streaming feels faster but costs the same. Batch is cheap but slow. The job is to know which corner the product needs and to measure all three.

questions.question: What is observability in the context of LLM systems?
questions.answer: The practice of designing your system so any cost, quality, or performance question can be answered from emitted telemetry without needing to redeploy. For LLM systems: structured per-request logs, dashboards rolled up by feature and user, alerts when burn rate or cache hit rate cross thresholds.

questions.question: What is the difference between a chat completion and an agent?
questions.answer: A chat completion is a single round-trip: prompt in, text out. An agent is a loop: model returns tool call, harness executes, result feeds back, model decides what's next. Cost profile differs dramatically. Single completion is bounded by max_tokens; agent's cost is bounded by budget policy.

questions.question: How do I count tokens before sending a request?
questions.answer: Each vendor provides a tokenizer. OpenAI: tiktoken library. Anthropic: client.messages.count_tokens endpoint. Gemini: count_tokens method on the model object. Counting before sending lets you reject oversize requests, choose between cheap and expensive models, and verify prompt-compression actually reduced count.

questions.question: What fields should a structured LLM log line contain?
questions.answer: Canonical schema: model, prompt_hash, input_tokens, output_tokens, cached_tokens, latency_ms, cost_usd, feature, user_id (hashed), outcome, trace_id, request_id. Optional: session_id, prompt_template_id, tool_call_count, stop_reason. Schema must support cost attribution by every dimension you care about.

questions.question: How does prompt caching work in the Anthropic API?
questions.answer: Mark a content block with cache_control: {type: ephemeral}. Anthropic stores the prefilled key-value cache for that prefix for ~5 minutes (or longer with 1-hour TTL option). Subsequent requests with same prefix bill cached portion at 10% of input rate. Response usage reports cache_creation_input_tokens and cache_read_input_tokens.

questions.question: What are the published per-million-token prices?
questions.answer: Always check vendor pricing page. Rough mental model: small/fast tier (Haiku, GPT-4o-mini, Gemini Flash) is $0.25-$1 per million input. Mid tier (Sonnet) is ~$3 per million input. Large tier (Opus, Gemini Ultra) is ~$15 per million. Output is 3-5x input rate. Cached input is 10% of input rate.

questions.question: What does the response usage object look like across vendors?
questions.answer: Anthropic: usage.input_tokens, usage.output_tokens, usage.cache_creation_input_tokens, usage.cache_read_input_tokens. OpenAI: usage.prompt_tokens, usage.completion_tokens, usage.total_tokens, prompt_tokens_details.cached_tokens. Gemini: usageMetadata.promptTokenCount, candidatesTokenCount, totalTokenCount. Field names differ; semantics align. A vendor-neutral logging layer must reconcile this.

questions.question: What is the difference between max_tokens and a stop sequence?
questions.answer: max_tokens is a hard upper bound on output tokens; the model is forced to stop when limit is hit, even mid-sentence. A stop sequence is a token pattern that, when generated, causes clean stop (e.g., </answer>). Use max_tokens as safety belt; stop sequences for graceful structural termination. Complementary, not alternatives.

questions.question: How does extended thinking affect token cost?
questions.answer: Extended thinking generates reasoning tokens billed at the output rate. Reasoning content is not visible to end user but appears in output token count. A thinking-heavy response can be 5-10x the cost of a non-thinking response on the same task. Reserve for problems where quality lift is worth it; cap thinking-token budget explicitly.

questions.question: What is JSON mode and how does it change cost?
questions.answer: A vendor feature constraining generation to valid JSON conforming to a supplied schema. Doesn't change per-token price but typically reduces output tokens (model can't pad with prose) and eliminates retry-on-parse-failure cost. Schema adds input tokens, so on small calls the net is sometimes a wash.

questions.question: What is the difference between Claude Haiku, Sonnet, and Opus?
questions.answer: Small, mid, large tiers of the Claude family. Haiku: fastest and cheapest, for classification, extraction, routing. Sonnet: workhorse mid tier for most production reasoning. Opus: largest, for hard reasoning where capability matters more than cost. Pricing ratios roughly Haiku 1x : Sonnet 12x : Opus 60x on input rate.

questions.question: What is the difference between GPT-4o, GPT-4o-mini, and the o-series?
questions.answer: GPT-4o is OpenAI's flagship general-purpose model. GPT-4o-mini is the small/fast variant for routing and high-volume work. The o-series (o1, o3) are reasoning models using extended internal thinking, billing those thinking tokens at output rate. Costs more per call but solves harder problems.

questions.question: What is the difference between Gemini Pro, Flash, and Ultra?
questions.answer: Flash: small/fast tier, analogous to Haiku and GPT-4o-mini. Pro: mid tier with the one-million-token context window. Ultra: largest tier. Defining Gemini feature is the long context window; Pro reliably handles a million input tokens, making it the natural choice for long-document analysis when cost can be justified.

questions.question: What is OpenTelemetry and should I use it for LLM logging?
questions.answer: A vendor-neutral observability standard with conventions for LLM call instrumentation under gen_ai semantic conventions. Using OTel means traces, metrics, logs work with any compliant backend (Honeycomb, Datadog, Grafana Tempo) avoiding lock-in. OTel LLM conventions cover most canonical schema fields out of the box.

questions.question: What is a prompt hash and why log it?
questions.answer: A stable hash (typically SHA-256, truncated to 16 hex chars) of the full prompt content, computed before the request and stored as a log field. Lets you group and aggregate by prompt without storing the prompt text. Useful for cost attribution by template, finding duplicate requests, keeping logs privacy-safe.

questions.question: What is a sample size calculation for an A/B test?
questions.answer: Determines how many requests each variant needs to detect a true effect of given size with given confidence. Inputs: baseline metric value, minimum detectable effect, statistical power (typically 0.8), significance level (typically 0.05). Without it you risk stopping too early or running too long.

questions.question: What is the difference between statistical power and statistical significance?
questions.answer: Significance (p-value) is the probability of observing your result if variants were actually identical; low p-value means result is unlikely noise. Power is the probability of detecting a true effect of a given size; high power means real wins are unlikely missed. Significance protects false positives; power protects false negatives.

questions.question: What is a guardrail metric?
questions.answer: A secondary measurement on an A/B test that protects against improvements in the primary metric coming at the cost of something you also care about. If primary metric is cost-per-request, guardrails are typically quality and latency. A 30% cost reduction that drops quality 10% is not a win.

questions.question: What is P95 token usage and why log it?
questions.answer: The 95th percentile of a distribution: the value below which 95% of observations fall. P95 input tokens shows your long-tail requests, where runaway agents and pathological prompts hide. Mean is misleading because a few 200K-token requests pull it up. Log P50, P95, and P99 for every cost-relevant metric.

questions.question: What is the difference between embeddings and tokens?
questions.answer: Tokens are integer IDs the model reads and writes. Embeddings are dense floating-point vectors (typically 1,536 to 3,072 dimensions) produced by an embedding model from text. Embeddings power RAG semantic search. Embeddings are billed in tokens (the input being embedded) at a separate, usually cheaper, embedding-model rate.

questions.question: What is reranking in RAG?
questions.answer: A second-pass scoring step in a RAG pipeline. Initial retrieval (vector search or BM25) returns top 50-100 candidates; a heavier cross-encoder reranker scores each candidate against the query and keeps top 3-5 to inject. Reranking dramatically improves retrieval precision, letting you inject fewer chunks for lower input-token cost without losing quality.

questions.question: What is conversation compaction?
questions.answer: Replacing the older portion of a long conversation with a shorter summary so total context stays under target size. Auto-compaction (harness does on threshold) is convenient but lossy. Manual compaction (user or Skill summarizes explicitly) is precise but requires intervention. Non-optional for any agent session running longer than dozens of turns.

questions.question: Why is my prompt cache hit rate lower than expected?
questions.answer: Most common cause: a silently invalidated cache key. Anthropic prompt caching requires byte-identical prefixes. Breakers include changed model ID, reordered tool list, date or timestamp in system prompt, non-deterministic JSON serialization that reorders keys, tool definition added at start instead of end. Print and diff actual prefix bytes between requests.

questions.question: Why is my agent session running up huge bills overnight?
questions.answer: Standard diagnosis is a pathological agent loop: model stuck repeating roughly the same tool call, each iteration replaying entire prior context as input. Look at session log for tool call iteration count, diversity of tool arguments across iterations, input-token growth curve. Structural fix is an agent budget policy.

questions.question: Why does my OpenAI bill not match my Anthropic bill on the same task?
questions.answer: Two most likely causes: tokenizer drift (same English text tokenizes to different counts on different vendors) and output verbosity differences (same instruction can produce 200 tokens on one model and 800 tokens on another). Always compare on cost-per-successful-outcome, not cost-per-call.

questions.question: Why are my cached tokens being charged at full rate?
questions.answer: Either cache entry expired (default Anthropic TTL is ~5 minutes between writes/reads on same prefix) or cache key was silently invalidated. Check response usage object: cache_read_input_tokens is count actually served from cache; everything else billed as input_tokens was uncacheable or a miss. Cache writes cost slightly more than uncached input.

questions.question: Why do my A/B test results keep flipping?
questions.answer: Almost always: insufficient sample size, peeking-at-the-data, or novelty effect. Insufficient sample means observed difference is within noise floor. Peeking means you keep checking and stopping when it looks good; fix with pre-registered stopping rule. Novelty effect: user behavior changes when something new appears and reverts after days.

questions.question: Why is my context window full when I haven't sent that much text?
questions.answer: A multi-turn agent session accumulates the full conversation history on every request. Add a 4,000-token system prompt, ten user messages, ten assistant responses, and tool-call/result pairs, and you can hit 50,000 input tokens on the eleventh turn even if each message was small. Fix: conversation compaction plus per-session budget.

questions.question: Why is my Skill not loading when I expect it to?
questions.answer: Most common cause: trigger description too generic. The harness can't tell when the Skill is relevant, so it loads on irrelevant tasks (false positives) or fails to load when needed (false negatives). Fix: rewrite trigger description with specific keywords, file types, or task verbs distinguishing this Skill's domain.

questions.question: Why are my output tokens so much higher than my input tokens?
questions.answer: Either model is genuinely producing a lot (long answers, code, JSON output) or using extended thinking generating reasoning tokens at output rate. Check response usage for thinking-token field. Fix: max_tokens cap, 'be concise' instruction, smaller reasoning budget, or routing easier requests to a non-thinking model.

questions.question: Why does my RAG pipeline cost so much per query?
questions.answer: Usually too many chunks at too large a size. Top-k=20 chunks at 1,000 tokens each is 20,000 input tokens injected per query, and most chunks aren't actually used. Fix: tighter pipeline with smaller chunks, smaller top-k, a reranker that lets you keep only top 3-5, and context pruning dropping chunks below quality threshold.

questions.question: Why does my cost dashboard show a spike at 2 AM every day?
questions.answer: Common culprits: nightly batch job running on synchronous API instead of batch; scheduled re-index re-embedding the full corpus; agent stuck retrying after rate limit. Investigation pattern: slice the spike by feature, then user, then model.

questions.question: Why is my batch job sitting in pending forever?
questions.answer: Each vendor has its own batch processing window. Anthropic and OpenAI typically deliver within 24 hours but can take longer under load; Gemini batch mode has its own SLA. Check batch job status object for explicit error. Common failures: malformed request line (whole job rejected), exceeded per-job request count, vendor queue depth.

questions.question: Why does my prompt sometimes hit the cache and sometimes miss?
questions.answer: Look for any per-request variability in the cached prefix: timestamp, request ID, user ID, randomized few-shot order, tool list whose order depends on a Python dict's iteration order. Cache invariants are the silent killer of hit rate; a single byte difference is a cache miss. Lock prefix down with deterministic serialization.

questions.question: When should I use prompt caching?
questions.answer: Whenever you have a stable prefix of at least 1,024 tokens reused across multiple requests within a few minutes. Strong fits: long system prompt shared by all users; tool-definition list shared by all calls in session; retrieved document block reused across follow-ups. Weak fits: prompts that change every request, single-shot calls, prefixes shorter than minimum.

questions.question: When should I use a batch API instead of synchronous calls?
questions.answer: Whenever workload tolerates hours of latency. Strong fits: nightly classification, bulk evaluation, embedding generation, offline analysis, large-scale data labeling. Weak fits: any user-facing path. The 50% discount on batch is large enough that 'could this run on batch?' should be a question asked of every recurring workload.

questions.question: When should I escalate from a cheap model to an expensive one?
questions.answer: When cheap model's confidence is low, when task is detected as hard at routing time, or when a downstream quality check fails. Escalation rule should be explicit and measurable: 'escalate when classifier confidence below 0.7' or 'escalate when JSON schema validation fails twice'. Never escalate 'just to be safe' or expensive becomes the floor.

questions.question: How do I write a good Skill trigger description?
questions.answer: Specific enough that the model can tell when the Skill is relevant from the description alone, short enough not to bloat always-loaded context. Lead with task verbs (generate, validate, deploy), name artifact types (p5.js MicroSim, structured log line, A/B test report), avoid generic adjectives. Test against representative tasks; measure false-positive and false-negative rates.

questions.question: How do I bound an agent session's cost?
questions.answer: Set explicit per-session token budget, loop iteration limit, and wall-clock limit, then write a graceful-degradation handler firing when any is exhausted: agent should summarize progress and stop, not silently fail or keep looping. Layer at harness level, not just prompt level: a budget enforced only by instructing model in system prompt is one the model can ignore.

questions.question: How do I choose between Anthropic, OpenAI, and Google for a given task?
questions.answer: Run a controlled benchmark on YOUR task with a fixed evaluation set and measure cost-per-successful-outcome, latency P95, and quality, then choose. Public benchmarks rank on tasks that may not match yours; vendor pricing alone doesn't capture verbosity differences. The course's vendor-pluralistic stance exists because the right answer for one team is wrong for another.

questions.question: How do I keep my LLM logs privacy-safe?
questions.answer: Hash user identifiers before logging. Redact known PII patterns (email, phone, SSN) from prompt content before logging, or skip logging prompt content entirely and log only the prompt hash. Log only what cost attribution needs. Apply retention policy deleting logs after the cost-investigation window closes (~90 days).

questions.question: How do I refactor a prose-heavy Skill into a script-backed one?
questions.answer: Identify deterministic portions (input validation, file generation, formatting, calculations) and move them into a bundled shell or Python script the Skill calls. Keep in model-facing prose only parts genuinely requiring model judgment: design decisions, narrative explanations, ambiguous parsing. Measure tokens before and after; target ~30% reduction.

questions.question: How do I run a credible A/B test on a prompt change?
questions.answer: Pre-register hypothesis, primary metric, guardrail metrics, sample size, and stopping rule BEFORE turning on the experiment. Split traffic randomly. Wait for planned sample size before peeking. Report effect size with confidence interval, not just p-value. If result is null, accept it instead of mining for a subgroup that 'won'.

questions.question: How do I attribute cost to features?
questions.answer: Tag every LLM call with a feature field at emit time; never try to attribute to features by parsing prompt content after the fact. Tag is set by calling code (usually a thin SDK wrapper) and travels through the log pipeline to the dashboard. Without the tag, attribution is unbounded archaeology; with it, attribution is a single GROUP BY.

questions.question: How do I know if a cost optimization actually worked?
questions.answer: Run a controlled A/B test, not a before-after comparison on the same time window, because traffic mix changes day to day and 'after the change' includes that drift. The A/B test should measure cost, quality, and latency simultaneously: a cost win that hides a quality regression is not a win. Report as confidence-interval-bounded effect size.

questions.question: How often should I review my token cost dashboard?
questions.answer: A short daily glance for anomaly detection (any unexpected spike, any cache hit-rate dip), a weekly deeper review by feature and user (where is spend growing or shrinking), a monthly review with engineering management (top cost drivers, optimization roadmap, ROI on shipped optimizations). Dashboards should be designed for that cadence.

questions.question: How do I design a vendor-neutral logging schema that works across Claude, OpenAI, and Gemini?
questions.answer: Define a single canonical schema with vendor-agnostic field names (input_tokens, output_tokens, cached_tokens, model, latency_ms, cost_usd) and write a thin adapter per vendor mapping the response usage object into that schema. Resolve field-name differences at adapter boundary, not in dashboard. Dashboard should never know which vendor produced a row.

questions.question: When does extended thinking pay for itself?
questions.answer: When the task is hard enough that the small/mid-tier non-thinking model fails more than ~15% of the time, and the cost of failure is high enough to justify 5-10x for higher success rate. For most production workloads (classification, extraction, summarization, routing) extended thinking is overkill and a non-thinking model with a tighter prompt wins on cost-per-successful-outcome. Test on your task with your quality metric.

questions.question: How do I architect a cost-aware multi-vendor router?
questions.answer: Three layers: a task classifier estimating difficulty and task type; a routing policy mapping (task, difficulty, budget) to (vendor, model); a fallback handler escalating on confidence-below-threshold or schema-validation-failure. Log every routing decision so you can audit. Plan for the policy to be wrong sometimes; measure escalation rate as a key metric and tune against cost-per-successful-outcome.

questions.question: How do I evaluate vendor lock-in risk when adopting Anthropic prompt caching or Gemini long context?
questions.answer: For each vendor-specific feature, ask three questions: cost lift if I adopt? migration time off? migration cost in tokens, engineering time, quality regression? Anthropic prompt caching has clean alternatives on OpenAI and Gemini, so adoption is low-risk. Gemini's one-million context has no equivalent on the others; adopting locks you in until they catch up. Document lock-in cost explicitly.

questions.question: How do I design a token budget policy for an autonomous coding harness?
questions.answer: Combine per-session token cap (e.g., 300,000 tokens), per-tool-call budget (e.g., 50 calls), wall-clock limit (e.g., 30 minutes), per-PR budget (cannot exceed N tokens on a single PR), and per-engineer weekly budget surfacing in a manager-reviewable report. Each limit has graceful degradation: summarize and stop, escalate to human, or write partial result.

questions.question: How do I build a continuous cost optimization loop for a production LLM system?
questions.answer: Instrument with structured logging on day one. Build dashboards surfacing top cost drivers by feature, user, model. Maintain an optimization backlog with hypotheses ranked by expected savings. Run one A/B test at a time with pre-registered hypothesis and stopping rule. Ship the winner, archive the loser. Repeat weekly. Compounding effect of modest weekly wins flattens the cost graph.

questions.question: How do I design a Skill from scratch with optimal token economics?
questions.answer: Start with a one-line trigger description naming task verb and artifact type. Write a concise body with only model-judgment-required steps. Move every deterministic step into a bundled script (validation, formatting, file I/O, math). Keep all reference material in bundled asset files loaded only when needed. Measure baseline tokens; target at least 30% reduction versus prose-only Skill.

questions.question: How do I balance the cost-quality-latency tradeoff for a user-facing product?
questions.answer: Pick the dimension your product most needs first (usually quality for hard task, latency for chat UI, cost for high-volume background job). Set hard floors on the other two. Measure all three on every change. When two changes both improve priority dimension, prefer the one not hitting the floors. Revisit priority quarterly: V1 quality-first may be V2 cost-first at scale.
